content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Kevin Riley O'Keefe 
155 Hollis Avenue 
No. 7 
Campbell, CA 
95008 
Dear Mr. O'Keefe: 
July 11, 1989 
Re: 
Our File No. G-89-317 
This is in response to the letter you directed to John H. 
Larson, Chairman of the Fair Political Practices commission. 
In the letter, you ask if something can be done to permit 18-
year-olds whose birthdays fall between the June primary election 
and the November general election to vote in both elections. 
The Commission has no jurisdiction over the laws which govern 
voting in California or Federal elections. 
We suggest that you 
contact your representatives in both the California Legislature 
and the United states Congress and, hopefully, they can 
satisfactorily address your question. 
KED:SH:ld 
Sincerely, 
Kathr.yYn E. Donovan 
Genera 
counse~

1/. 
':x{r [)7; ,~c;If/z"~ 
BY: 
Scott Hallabrin 
Counsel, Legal Division 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804#0807 • (916) 322#5660 


Mr. John H. Larson 
428 J st., Suite #800 
Sacramento, Ca. 
95815 
Mr. Larson: 
Kevin Riley O'Keeffe 
155 Hollis ave., #7 
Campbell, Ca. 
95008 
Their is an inequity in the way Californians vote that I would like 
to express to you, an inequity that has been done away with in 
nearly a dozen other states. This inequity is embodied by the fact 
that people whose 18th birthday fall between the June primary 
election and the November general election are only allowed to vote 
in the general election, which is unfair and totally illogical as 
the primary election is merely a part of the same process with is 
completed in November. To allow someone to vote only in the general 
election but not in the primary election, even if the primary 
involves candidates for the nomination of the party to which the 
indiviual belongs, is the same as telling the person that he/she 
will have to wait for the next election to participate fully in the 
democratic process. If a person's 18th birthday falls just before an 
election, we should be congratulating them for being able to begin 
inputing their ideas into the political world, as opposed to 
penalizing them for being youthful and denying them the right to 
participate in the selection of candidates which occurs in June. 
s 
Keef 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Kevin Riley 0/Keeffe 
155 Hollis Avenue, #7 
Campbell, CA 
95008 
Dear Mr. 0/Keeffe: 
May 24, 1989 
Re: 
Letter No. 89-317 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act 
was received on MAY 17, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices 
commission. 
If you have any questions about your advice request, 
you may contact Scott Hallabrin an attorney in the Legal Division, 
directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or 
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21 
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. 
If more 
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response 
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to 
information needed. 
If your request is for informal assistance, 
we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission 
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
KED:plh 
Very trq y yours, 
// 
i}J}; '/ ~ 
~c jl( /. {t/{c :'Z"'~ 
Rathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
uite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804,0807 • (916)322,5660

sections.question: In the letter, you ask if something can be done to permit 18-year-olds whose birthdays fall between the June primary election and the November general election to vote in both elections.
sections.conclusion: The Commission has no jurisdiction over the laws which govern voting in California or Federal elections. We suggest that you contact your representatives in both the California Legislature and the United states Congress and, hopefully, they can satisfactorily address your question.
sections.question_synthetic: Can the FPPC permit 18-year-olds whose birthdays fall between the June primary and November general election to vote in both elections, rather than only the general election?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC lacks jurisdiction over voting laws in California or Federal elections, and recommends that the requester contact their representatives in the California Legislature and U.S. Congress to address this policy concern.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes incoming request letter and acknowledgment letter along with FPPC response letter. The response clearly states lack of jurisdiction. All three letters are present in the provided document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: In the letter, you ask if something can be done to permit 18-year-olds whose birthdays fall between the June primary election and the November general election to vote in both elections.

CONCLUSION: The Commission has no jurisdiction over the laws which govern voting in California or Federal elections. We suggest that you contact your representatives in both the California Legislature and the United states Congress and, hopefully, they can satisfactorily address your question.

embedding.summary: On July 11, 1989, the FPPC responded to Kevin Riley O'Keefe's inquiry about whether 18-year-olds with birthdays between the June primary and November general election could vote in both elections, declining jurisdiction and referring him to legislative representatives.
